one_line_takeaway: A survey/position paper that organizes agentic-AI security research into a six-family threat/defense taxonomy and argues each family is amplified at 'Agentic Web' scale, proposing identity/authorization, provenance, and ecosystem-response as the missing primitives -- it reports no original experiments, attacks, or datasets.
key_finding: There is no empirical/quantitative finding — the paper's contribution is organizational/conceptual. Its main claims are: (1) existing agentic-AI security work can be cleanly mapped onto a six-family threat/defense taxonomy tied to agent architecture components; (2) each of these threat/defense families is amplified or qualitatively changed when agents operate at web scale via delegation chains and protocols such as MCP, because compromises can propagate across agent-to-agent communication and third-party tool/service providers introduce supply-chain-like risk; and (3) three system-level primitives (identity/authorization, provenance/traceability, ecosystem-level response) are currently missing and required to secure a future 'Agentic Web.'
core_contribution: This is a survey/position paper (not an empirical study) that proposes a component-aligned threat taxonomy for agentic AI security — six threat families (Prompt Abuse, Environment Injection, Memory Attacks, Toolchain Abuse, Model Tampering, Agent Network Attacks) each paired with a corresponding defense family (Prompt Hardening, Model Robustness, Tool Control, Runtime Monitoring, Continuous Red Teaming, Protocol Security) — and then explicitly lifts each category to a proposed 'Agentic Web' setting via a per-section 'Agentic Web implication' paragraph. The paper's own novel contribution is this organizing taxonomy plus the transition-oriented framing (Sections 3-5 pairing each single-agent category with its web-scale manifestation) and a concluding argument for three new system primitives needed at web scale: (1) interoperable machine identity/authorization, (2) provenance and traceability of delegation chains and tool outputs, and (3) ecosystem-level response mechanisms (quarantine, revocation, recovery). It introduces no new attacks, defenses, datasets, or experiments of its own; nearly every specific claim in the body (e.g., AgentPoison, StruQ, ProGENT, AgentDojo, PromptInfection) is a citation to prior work being surveyed, not a result the authors produced.
threat_model: Not a single fixed threat model — the paper is a taxonomy covering multiple threat classes drawn from cited literature, organized by where an adversary injects influence into the LLM-agent pipeline (Figure 1): user inputs (prompt abuse/jailbreak), external environment content such as web pages/documents/UI (indirect/environment injection), agent memory (poisoning, data contamination, privacy leakage), tools/APIs (toolchain abuse, compromised tool outputs), the model/policy itself (backdoors, hidden triggers), and inter-agent communication (prompt propagation across multi-agent networks). The paper's own extension is a 'Agentic Web' escalation of this same threat model: it argues threats become networked across delegation chains between User Agents and Service Agents, trust must become an explicit machine-checkable primitive (vs. implicit for human users), and the dominant attack surface shifts to protocol/tool ecosystems (e.g., Model Context Protocol) with supply-chain-like third-party risk.
limitations: As a survey/position paper, it presents no original threat models, attacks, defenses, or evaluation data of its own — every quantitative claim, dataset, or benchmark result belongs to the cited works, not to this paper. The 'Agentic Web' concept and its associated architecture (Figure 3: User Agents / Service Agents / Trust & Security Layer) are largely aspirational/conceptual rather than grounded in deployed systems or measurements; the paper itself states this shift 'envisions' a future internet. The taxonomy's category boundaries are acknowledged by the authors to overlap in practice ('These categories overlap in practice, but they form a clean basis for analysis'). No systematic literature-search methodology (e.g., search terms, inclusion/exclusion criteria, coverage period) is described, so the survey's comprehensiveness cannot be independently assessed from the text provided.
